title: Garlic naan
subtitle: Leavened flatbread blistered with garlic butter
excerpt: Soft, slightly chewy leavened flatbread cooked in a very hot pan until blistered and charred in spots, then brushed with garlic-infused butter. The naan dough uses yoghurt and a small amount of yeast for a soft, pliable texture with a slight tang. The garlic butter applied straight after cooking soaks into the hot bread and perfumes it throughout.
sourceNotes: Naan is a leavened flatbread cooked in a tandoor, traditionally made with white flour and yoghurt. The British curry-house garlic naan (brushed with garlic butter after cooking) became the most popular naan variant on British menus from the 1980s onward. Domestic versions use a hot dry pan or griddle.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked naans, separated by paper. Reheat directly in a hot pan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dough can be made up to 4 hours ahead and refrigerated after the first rise.

content.text: Naan dough is soft (softer than bread dough) and needs a resting period for the yeast to activate and the gluten to relax. A 60-minute rest at room temperature is the minimum; the dough should visibly puff. Don't rush this stage: rushed dough is dense and doesn't stretch properly when rolled.

The pan must be as hot as possible. A dry cast-iron or non-stick pan at maximum heat gives the characteristic blistered surface. The naan goes in dry (no oil) and cooks for about 60 to 90 seconds per side. The dough should puff up dramatically in places and char in spots. If the surface barely colours in 2 minutes, the pan is not hot enough.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{fast-action-yeast}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Add the {{plain-yoghurt}}, {{milk}}, and {{vegetable-oil}}. Mix to a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead on a floured surface for 5 minutes until smooth and elastic. Place in an oiled bowl, cover, and leave in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled in size. Make the garlic butter by combining the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{garlic}}, and {{coriander}} if using.

Divide the risen dough into 4 equal pieces. Roll each piece on a floured surface into an oval about 25 cm long and 4 to 5 mm thick. Heat a dry cast-iron or heavy non-stick pan over the highest heat possible. Cook each naan for 60 to 90 seconds on the first side until bubbles appear and the underside has char spots. Flip and cook for a further 60 seconds. Brush generously with the garlic butter as soon as the naan comes off the heat. Stack on a plate and cover with a cloth to keep warm while cooking the remainder.

items.cause: Yeast not active or dough not rested long enough
items.fix: Check the yeast is fresh. The dough must visibly double in size before it is rolled — if it hasn't risen after 1 hour, it may be a cold room; move to a warmer location and wait
The pan must be properly smoking before the first naan goes in. Allow 5 minutes of preheating on maximum heat

items.cause: Dough not rested or too cold
items.fix: If refrigerating the dough, bring it to room temperature for 30 minutes before rolling. Cold dough is tight and resistant

items.cause: Raw garlic applied to the butter without softening
items.fix: Use very finely minced or grated garlic, which disperses evenly. Applying to the hot bread softens the rawness slightly — for a milder result, briefly warm the butter and garlic together before brushing

content.text: Garlic naan is the most ordered bread on the British Indian menu, having displaced plain naan as the default choice from the 1980s onward. The garlic-butter finish adds a specific richness that works well alongside the spiced sauces of main dishes. At home the main variable is heat — a domestic hob cannot match the temperature of a restaurant tandoor, but a cast-iron pan on its highest setting produces a credible result with the blistered, charred surface that makes naan distinct from other flatbreads.
